report:
## Profitability

Both META and GOOGL have demonstrated exceptional and accelerating profitability over the 2022–2025 period, though GOOGL operates at a materially larger absolute scale. GOOGL's net income grew from $59.97B in FY2022 [GOOGL:Q3] to $132.17B in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q6], while operating income expanded from $74.84B [GOOGL:Q7] to $129.04B [GOOGL:Q10] over the same span. META's trajectory is arguably more dramatic in percentage terms: net income surged from $23.20B in FY2022 [META:Q0] to a peak of $62.36B in FY2024 [META:Q2], before a modest dip to $60.46B in FY2025 [META:Q3], while operating income continued climbing to $83.28B in FY2025 [META:Q7]. GOOGL's operating income of $129.04B [GOOGL:Q10] is roughly 55% larger than META's $83.28B [META:Q7] in FY2025, reflecting GOOGL's significantly broader revenue base of $402.84B [GOOGL:Q2] versus META's more concentrated advertising business. Both companies reflect the high-margin nature of digital advertising and platform businesses, but GOOGL's absolute profit levels remain substantially higher.

**META:** META executed a remarkable profitability turnaround, with net income nearly tripling from $23.20B in FY2022 [META:Q0] to $62.36B in FY2024 [META:Q2], driven by aggressive cost discipline and advertising revenue growth. Operating income reached $83.28B in FY2025 [META:Q7], even as net income dipped slightly to $60.46B [META:Q3], suggesting some below-the-line headwinds. The multi-year trajectory reflects a highly efficient, predominantly advertising-driven business with expanding margins.

**GOOGL:** GOOGL has delivered consistently strong and growing profitability at scale, with net income rising from $59.97B in FY2022 [GOOGL:Q3] to $132.17B in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q6], and operating income reaching $129.04B [GOOGL:Q10] on revenues of $402.84B [GOOGL:Q2]. The close alignment between operating and net income growth signals disciplined cost management and limited non-operating drag. GOOGL's absolute profit levels dwarf most peers and reflect the compounding advantages of its search, cloud, and advertising ecosystem.

---

## Growth

Both META and GOOGL are exhibiting strong top-line growth, though META's trajectory appears to be accelerating more sharply into early 2026 while GOOGL shows broad-based, steady expansion across its segments. META's total revenue reached $200.97B for full-year 2025, with Family of Apps advertising revenue growing 22% year-over-year [META:S1], and regional momentum accelerating into Q1 2026 with Europe and Rest of World up 39% and 40%, respectively, versus the same period in 2025 [META:S3]. GOOGL's nine-month 2025 revenues showed U.S. growth of 13% and APAC growth of 18% on a constant-currency basis [GOOGL:S2], while Google Cloud revenues surged $15.5B from 2024 to 2025, driven by infrastructure and platform services [GOOGL:S3]. META is also investing heavily in growth, with R&D expenses rising 35% in Q3 2025 and engineering headcount up 11% year-over-year [META:S0], signaling confidence in sustaining its AI-driven expansion. GOOGL similarly highlighted subscription revenue growth across YouTube and Google One as a key driver [GOOGL:S3]. Both companies face some foreign exchange headwinds, with META noting an unfavorable FX impact on full-year 2025 revenue [META:S4] and GOOGL flagging FX effects particularly from the Argentine peso [GOOGL:S2].

**META:** META delivered full-year 2025 total revenue of approximately $200.97B, with advertising revenue growing 22% and non-advertising (Other) revenue surging 50% [META:S1]. Growth is accelerating into 2026, with Q1 2026 regional revenue up 29–40% across all geographies versus Q1 2025 [META:S3], supported by aggressive AI-focused R&D investment and 11% engineering headcount growth [META:S0].

**GOOGL:** GOOGL is growing steadily across all major segments, with nine-month 2025 constant-currency revenue up 13% in the U.S. and 18% in APAC [GOOGL:S2], and Google Cloud revenues rising $15.5B year-over-year driven by infrastructure and platform services [GOOGL:S3]. Subscription growth across YouTube and Google One is an additional tailwind, though the evidence base for precise full-year 2025 total revenue figures is somewhat thinner than for META.

---

## Leverage

META and GOOGL have followed strikingly different leverage trajectories over the past three years. META entered FY2023 with zero long-term debt [META:Q0] and has since aggressively built a debt stack, reaching ~$9.9B at end-FY2023 [META:Q1], ~$18.4B at end-FY2024 [META:Q2], and ~$28.8B at end-FY2025 [META:Q3] — nearly tripling in two years. Despite this rapid debt accumulation, META's equity base of ~$125.7B [META:Q11] and a cash position that surged to ~$41.9B by FY2025 [META:Q15] keep net leverage modest; net debt at year-end FY2025 is roughly -$13B (i.e., still net cash). GOOGL, by contrast, maintained a relatively stable and modest long-term debt load of ~$15.3B in FY2022 [GOOGL:Q0] declining to ~$13.0B by FY2024 [GOOGL:Q2], but then reported a notably higher total long-term debt figure of ~$49.1B in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q3] — though the noncurrent portion remained far lower at ~$10.9B [GOOGL:Q7], suggesting a significant current or short-term component in that figure. GOOGL's equity base is substantially larger at ~$256.1B [GOOGL:Q11], and its cash of ~$23.5B [GOOGL:Q15] provides meaningful offset. On a debt-to-equity basis, both companies remain conservatively levered relative to their equity, but META's rapid ramp-up from zero debt to ~$28.8B in three years represents a meaningful structural shift in its capital structure, while GOOGL's underlying noncurrent leverage has historically been low and stable.

**META:** META transformed its balance sheet from entirely debt-free in FY2022 [META:Q0] to carrying ~$28.8B in long-term debt by FY2025 [META:Q3], a deliberate shift toward a more leveraged capital structure likely tied to AI infrastructure investment and shareholder returns. However, with equity of ~$125.7B [META:Q11] and cash that jumped to ~$41.9B [META:Q15], the company remains in a net cash position and the absolute leverage ratio is still manageable.

**GOOGL:** GOOGL maintained a conservative and gradually declining long-term debt load through FY2024, falling to ~$13.0B [GOOGL:Q2] against a large equity base of ~$251.6B [GOOGL:Q10], reflecting minimal financial leverage. The FY2025 total long-term debt figure of ~$49.1B [GOOGL:Q3] appears inflated by a current portion, as the noncurrent component stood at only ~$10.9B [GOOGL:Q7]; with equity of ~$256.1B [GOOGL:Q11] and ~$23.5B in cash [GOOGL:Q15], GOOGL's balance sheet leverage remains low in structural terms.

---

GOOGL, by contrast, maintained a relatively stable and modest long-term debt load of ~$15.3B in FY2022 [GOOGL:Q0] declining to ~$13.0B by FY2024 [GOOGL:Q2], but then reported a sharp jump to ~$49.1B in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q3] — though the noncurrent portion remained far lower at ~$10.9B [GOOGL:Q7], suggesting a significant current/short-term component in that figure. On a debt-to-equity basis, both companies remain conservatively levered relative to their equity, but META's rapid ramp-up from zero debt to ~$28.8B in three years represents a meaningful structural shift in its capital structure, while GOOGL's leverage has historically been low and stable.

Both META and GOOGL have demonstrated exceptional and accelerating profitability over the 2022–2025 period, though GOOGL operates at a materially larger absolute scale. GOOGL's net income grew from $59.97B in FY2022 [GOOGL:Q3] to $132.17B in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q6], while operating income expanded from $74.84B [GOOGL:Q7] to $129.04B [GOOGL:Q10] over the same span. META's trajectory is arguably more dramatic in percentage terms: net income surged from $23.20B in FY2022 [META:Q0] to a peak of $62.36B in FY2024 [META:Q2], before a modest dip to $60.46B in FY2025 [META:Q3], while operating income continued climbing to $83.28B in FY2025 [META:Q7]. GOOGL's operating income of $129.04B [GOOGL:Q10] is roughly 55% larger than META's $83.28B [META:Q7] in FY2025, reflecting GOOGL's significantly broader revenue base of $402.84B [GOOGL:Q2] versus META's more concentrated advertising business. Both companies reflect the high-margin nature of digital advertising and platform businesses, but GOOGL's absolute profit levels remain substantially higher.
